title-group.article-title: Letter to the editor regarding: Efficacy of esketamine in reducing nausea and vomiting after anesthesia: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials

contrib-group.aff: Department of Anesthesiology, Affiliated Hospital of Jiaxing University, Jiaxing, China

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

funding-group.funding-statement: Supported by Zhejiang Provincial Clinical Key Specialties-Anesthesiology [2023-ZJZK-001].

body.p: We read with interest the systematic review and meta-analysis by Tang et al. evaluating the efficacy of perioperative esketamine in reducing postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) [1]. The authors should be commended for synthesizing a substantial body of randomized evidence on an increasingly adopted anesthetic adjunct. Nevertheless, several methodological and interpretative aspects merit further discussion, as they bear directly on the clinical meaning of the reported findings.

First, the meta-analysis treats nausea and vomiting largely as parallel manifestations of a single outcome. While often grouped under the umbrella of PONV, these symptoms differ in pathophysiology, temporal distribution, and responsiveness to pharmacological interventions [2]. Early postoperative nausea is frequently linked to anesthetic agents and vestibular sensitivity, whereas vomiting particularly beyond the immediate recovery phase has been associated with opioid exposure and delayed gastrointestinal motility, reflecting distinct neurophysiological pathways in PONV pathogenesis [3]. Pooling these outcomes across heterogeneous time windows may obscure clinically relevant distinctions and risks overgeneralizing esketamine’s antiemetic profile.

Second, although the authors report a reduction in both nausea and vomiting, the potential indirect mechanism underlying this effect warrants closer scrutiny. Esketamine’s opioid-sparing properties are well established, and postoperative opioid consumption remains one of the strongest modifiable risk factors for PONV [4]. However, the current analysis does not adequately disentangle whether the observed reduction in PONV reflects a direct antiemetic effect or is mediated primarily through reduced opioid requirements. Without stratified analyses according to postoperative analgesic consumption or formal mediation-oriented discussion, the findings may be misinterpreted as supporting an intrinsic antiemetic role of esketamine.

Third, the comparator arms across included trials encompass a wide spectrum of anesthetic strategies, ranging from saline placebo to opioid-based or dexmedetomidine-containing regimens. Such conceptual heterogeneity extends beyond statistical variability and challenges the clinical interpretability of pooled estimates. In this context, subgroup analyses based solely on surgical type or geographic region may be insufficient to address differences in baseline PONV risk and antiemetic co-interventions. Greater emphasis on anesthetic strategy–specific comparisons could have strengthened the translational relevance of the results.

Finally, the reported prolongation of anesthesia recovery time and PACU stay deserves a more integrated discussion within contemporary perioperative care models. In enhanced recovery pathways, reductions in PONV must be weighed against delayed emergence and resource utilization. The coexistence of these opposing effects raises important questions regarding patient-centered benefit, particularly in ambulatory or fast-track surgical settings.

In summary, while this meta-analysis provides valuable aggregated data suggesting a potential role for esketamine in PONV reduction, cautious interpretation is warranted. Future evidence syntheses may benefit from a clearer distinction between nausea and vomiting phenotypes, more granular consideration of opioid-sparing pathways, and closer alignment with modern recovery-oriented anesthetic goals.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest related to this work.

ref.mixed-citation: Tang L, Qu S, Du Z, et al. Efficacy of esketamine in reducing nausea and vomiting after anesthesia: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. Ann Med. 2026;58(1):2604409. doi: 10.1080/07853890.2025.2604409.41454547 PMC12777928

Gan TJ, Jin Z, Ayad S, et al. Fifth consensus guidelines for the management of postoperative nausea and vomiting: executive summary. Anesth Analg. 2025. Epub ahead of print. doi: 10.1213/ANE.0000000000007816.41237407
Kovac AL. Pathophysiology and risk factors for postoperative nausea and vomiting in adults and children. BJA Educ. 2025;25(6):234–239. doi: 10.1016/j.bjae.2025.02.003.40496204 PMC12146002
Apfel CC, Heidrich FM, Jukar-Rao S, et al. Evidence-based analysis of risk factors for postoperative nausea and vomiting. Br J Anaesth. 2012;109(5):742–753. doi: 10.1093/bja/aes276.23035051
